title: How to Prepare Your Home Before Movers Arrive
excerpt: Pre-move home prep: timeline, pathways, zones, appliances, bags, pets and kids, night-before and morning sweep, keys, weather.
content:
How to prepare your home before movers arrive

Preparation converts chaos into throughput. When the truck pulls up, crews should lift, pad, and load—not wait for you to finish packing or hunt for keys.

Two weeks before: inventory and access

  • Finalize written estimate; reconfirm date and crew count.
  • Submit ID lists to compounds that require them.
  • Reserve elevator and loading bay.

One week before: packing state

  • Non-daily rooms packed and labeled by destination.
  • Fragile marked on two sides.
  • Disassemble only what your contract assigns to you.

Clear pathways

Hallways free of trip hazards; rugs rolled or secured; outdoor path to truck unobstructed.

Designate zones

Do-not-move area for valuables, documents, and Open First items. Tape an X on floors if helpful.

Appliances

Fridge defrosted and dry if moving; washer disconnected per manual; drain lines handled safely.

Personal bags

Medications, chargers, keys, and a change of clothes in bags that never touch the truck.

Kids and pets

Plan childcare or closed safe rooms during peak load; crates for animals.

Night before

  • Strip beds; keep one linen set accessible.
  • Charge phones; coffee supplies in Open First box.
  • Trash out; surfaces wiped where dust complicates packing.

Morning of: final sweep

  • Closets, balconies, storage cages checked.
  • Water for crew path to bathroom.
  • Walk fragile flags once with the lead.

Parking and keys ready

Keys on a numbered ring; parking passes spelled like official IDs.

Weather prep

Mats at entry if rain; plastic sheeting for marble lobbies if required.

Photos for baseline

Snap electronics and furniture condition before wrap—helps any claim conversation stay factual.

Trash and perishables

Empty trash; run down freezer inventory; avoid last-minute fridge surprises.

Curtains, rods, and wall hardware

Remove rods and patch plans if your lease requires; bag hardware labeled by room so rehang is fast.

Gas cylinders and cookers

Follow safety rules for disconnect; never improvise sealed lines—crews may refuse loose tanks on truck.

Outdoor and balcony items

Plan early for planters, grills, and drying racks—they surprise volume surveys.

Conclusion

When your home is staged, labeled, and secure before arrival, movers spend time moving—not waiting. That is the cheapest and safest version of moving day.
author: Active Movers Team
